section.title: Warnings and Watchfulness
section.sectionRationale: Still on the road to Jerusalem, Jesus warns His disciples against the hidden leaven of hypocrisy, calls them to fear God and confess Christ boldly, and exposes the folly of greed in the rich man who stored up goods but was not rich toward God. He then frees His people from anxiety with the Father who feeds ravens and clothes lilies, urges them to seek the kingdom and treasure in heaven, and calls them to stay dressed and watchful for the Master's sudden return. Integrity, trust, and readiness mark the waiting people of God.
section.thesis: Because the Master will return at an hour no one expects, His people are to live without hypocrisy or greed, without anxious grasping, seeking first His kingdom and keeping their lamps burning as servants ready to open the door the moment He knocks.
section.frameNote: Six movements: the warning against hypocrisy and the call to fearless confession; the parable of the rich fool; the cure for anxiety; treasure in heaven; readiness for the Master's return; and reading the present time.

verses.text: In the meantime, a crowd of many thousands had gathered, so that they were trampling one another. Jesus began to speak first to His disciples: “Beware of the leaven of the Pharisees, which is {{hypokrisis|hypocrisy}}.
There is nothing concealed that will not be disclosed, and nothing hidden that will not be made known.
What you have spoken in the dark will be heard in the daylight, and what you have whispered in the inner rooms will be proclaimed from the housetops.
I tell you, My friends, do not be afraid of those who kill the body and after that can do no more.
But I will show you whom you should fear: Fear the One who, after you have been killed, has authority to throw you into hell. Yes, I tell you, fear Him!
Are not five sparrows sold for two pennies? Yet not one of them is forgotten by God.
And even the very hairs of your head are all numbered. So do not be afraid; you are worth more than many sparrows.
I tell you, everyone who confesses Me before men, the Son of Man will also confess him before the angels of God.
But whoever denies Me before men will be denied before the angels of God.
And everyone who speaks a word against the Son of Man will be forgiven, but whoever blasphemes against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven.
When you are brought before synagogues, rulers, and authorities, do not worry about how to defend yourselves or what to say.
For at that time the Holy Spirit will teach you what you should say.”

units.label: The parable of the rich fool

verses.text: Someone in the crowd said to Him, “Teacher, tell my brother to divide the inheritance with me.”
But Jesus replied, “Man, who appointed Me judge or executor between you?”
And He said to them, “Watch out! Guard yourselves against every form of {{pleonexia|greed}}, for one’s life does not consist in the abundance of his possessions.”
Then He told them a parable: “The ground of a certain rich man produced an abundance.
So he thought to himself, ‘What shall I do, since I have nowhere to store my crops?’
Then he said, ‘This is what I will do: I will tear down my barns and will build bigger ones, and there I will store up all my grain and my goods.
Then I will say to myself, “You have plenty of good things laid up for many years. Take it easy. Eat, drink, and be merry!”’
But God said to him, ‘You fool! This very night your life will be required of you. Then who will own what you have accumulated?’
This is how it will be for anyone who stores up treasure for himself but is not rich toward God.”

units.label: Do not be anxious
units.note: consider the ravens and the lilies

verses.text: Then Jesus said to His disciples, “Therefore I tell you, do not {{merimnao|worry}} about your life, what you will eat, or about your body, what you will wear.
For life is more than food, and the body more than clothes.
Consider the ravens: They do not sow or reap, they have no storehouse or barn; yet God feeds them. How much more valuable you are than the birds!
Who of you by worrying can add a single hour to his life?
So if you cannot do such a small thing, why do you worry about the rest?
Consider how the lilies grow: They do not labor or spin. Yet I tell you, not even Solomon in all his glory was adorned like one of these.
If that is how God clothes the grass of the field, which is here today and tomorrow is thrown into the furnace, how much more will He clothe you, O you of little faith!
And do not be concerned about what you will eat or drink. Do not worry about it.
For the Gentiles of the world strive after all these things, and your Father knows that you need them.
But seek His kingdom, and these things will be added unto you.

units.label: Treasure in heaven
units.note: where your treasure is, there your heart will be

verses.text: Do not be afraid, little flock, for your Father is pleased to give you the kingdom.
Sell your possessions and give to the poor. Provide yourselves with purses that will not wear out, an inexhaustible treasure in heaven, where no thief approaches and no moth destroys.
For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also.
Be dressed for service and keep your lamps burning.
Then you will be like servants waiting for their master to return from the wedding banquet, so that when he comes and knocks, they can open the door for him at once.

Blessed are those servants whom the master finds {{gregoreo|on watch}} when he returns. Truly I tell you, he will dress himself to serve and will have them recline at the table, and he himself will come and wait on them.
Even if he comes in the second or third watch of the night and finds them alert, those servants will be blessed.
But understand this: If the homeowner had known at what hour the thief was coming, he would not have let his house be broken into.
You also must be ready, because the Son of Man will come at an hour you do not expect.”
“Lord,” said Peter, “are You addressing this parable to us, or to everyone else as well?”
And the Lord answered, “Who then is the faithful and wise manager, whom the master puts in charge of his servants to give them their portion at the proper time?
Blessed is that servant whose master finds him doing so when he returns.
Truly I tell you, he will put him in charge of all his possessions.
But suppose that servant says in his heart, ‘My master will be a long time in coming,’ and he begins to beat the menservants and maidservants, and to eat and drink and get drunk.
The master of that servant will come on a day he does not expect and at an hour he does not anticipate. Then he will cut him to pieces and assign him a place with the unbelievers.
That servant who knows his master’s will but does not get ready or follow his instructions will be beaten with many blows.

But the one who unknowingly does things worthy of punishment will be beaten with few blows. From everyone who has been given much, much will be required; and from him who has been entrusted with much, even more will be demanded.

units.label: Division and reading the present time

verses.text: I have come to ignite a fire on the earth, and how I wish it were already kindled!
But I have a baptism to undergo, and how distressed I am until it is accomplished!
Do you think that I have come to bring peace to the earth? No, I tell you, but division.
From now on, five in one household will be divided, three against two and two against three.
They will be divided, father against son and son against father, mother against daughter and daughter against mother, mother-in-law against daughter-in-law and daughter-in-law against mother-in-law.”
Then Jesus said to the crowds, “As soon as you see a cloud rising in the west, you say, ‘A shower is coming,’ and that is what happens.
And when the south wind blows, you say, ‘It will be hot,’ and it is.
You hypocrites! You know how to interpret the appearance of the earth and sky. Why don’t you know how to interpret the present time?
And why don’t you judge for yourselves what is right?

Make every effort to reconcile with your adversary while you are on your way to the magistrate. Otherwise, he may drag you off to the judge, and the judge may hand you over to the officer, and the officer may throw you into prison.
I tell you, you will not get out until you have paid the very last penny.”

hypokrisis.gloss: Hypocrisy, play-acting, the pretense of a stage actor wearing a mask. Jesus calls it the leaven of the Pharisees, a hidden influence that spreads. Because nothing will stay hidden, the disciple is to abandon the mask and live one, honest life before God.

pleonexia.gloss: Greed, covetousness, the grasping desire for more. Jesus warns against every form of it, for a person's life is not made up of possessions. The rich fool embodies pleonexia, hoarding for himself while poor toward God.

merimnao.gloss: To be anxious, to worry, to be pulled apart by care. Jesus repeatedly forbids this anxious striving over food and clothing, pointing to a Father who feeds ravens and clothes lilies and knows exactly what His children need.

gregoreo.gloss: To stay awake, to keep watch, to be alert. The blessed servant is the one the returning master finds on watch, dressed for service with lamp burning. Watchfulness is not nervous dread but faithful, ready love awaiting the Master.

basic.context: Before a huge crowd Jesus first warns His disciples against the hidden leaven of hypocrisy, telling them to fear God rather than men and to confess Him boldly, trusting the Spirit to speak through them. When a man asks Him to settle an inheritance, Jesus warns against greed and tells of a rich man who built bigger barns and died that very night, not rich toward God. He then frees His disciples from anxiety: the Father who feeds ravens and clothes lilies will surely care for them, so they should seek His kingdom and lay up treasure in heaven. Finally He calls them to stay dressed and watchful, lamps burning, for the Master will return at an unexpected hour, and He rebukes the crowd for reading the weather but not the present time.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the chapter as one sustained call to live now in light of the coming Master. Hypocrisy, greed, and anxiety are all forms of living as though God were absent or the future were ours to secure. Over against them Jesus sets the fear of God, trust in the Father, treasure in heaven, and watchful readiness. The parables of the rich fool and the waiting servants frame the two ways.
basic.originalLanguages: The warnings target hypokrisis, the actor's mask, and pleonexia, grasping greed. The cure for care is to refuse merimnao, anxious worry, and trust the Father. And the posture of the waiting church is gregoreo, to stay awake and alert for the Master's knock.
basic.typology: The Master who returns from the wedding banquet and, astonishingly, girds Himself to serve His watchful servants points to Christ, who came serving and will come again. His baptism of distress is the cross He must undergo.
basic.christ: Jesus is the One to be confessed before men, the Son of Man who will acknowledge or deny us before the angels. He is the returning Master whose hour is unknown, the servant-Lord who waits on His people, and the One who came to cast fire on the earth and to be baptized in suffering.
basic.god: The Father numbers the hairs of our heads and forgets not a single sparrow, feeds the ravens and clothes the lilies, and is pleased to give His little flock the kingdom. He is also the God to be feared, who judges the heart and before whom nothing stays hidden.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 6 parallels the teaching on anxiety and treasure. Philippians 4 turns anxiety into prayer. Colossians 3 names greed as idolatry. 1 Thessalonians 5 calls the church to stay awake for the day of the Lord.
basic.oneStory: The waiting people of God live now in light of the returning Master: free from hypocrisy, greed, and anxiety, seeking first the kingdom, storing treasure in heaven, and keeping their lamps burning until the Son of Man comes at an hour they do not expect.

context.literary: The chapter belongs to Luke's travel narrative and gathers teaching for disciples living between Jesus' first coming and His return. It moves from warning to parable to comfort to exhortation, bound together by the theme of readiness. The rich fool and the watchful servants are among Luke's distinctive parables of possessions and vigilance.
context.historical: First-century wealth was largely stored in crops and goods, so the rich fool's bigger barns were the obvious dream of security. Servants awaited a master's late return from a wedding feast, keeping lamps trimmed through the night watches. Reading the western clouds off the Mediterranean and the hot south wind off the desert were everyday Palestinian weather signs.
context.canonical: The call not to be anxious but to seek the kingdom fulfills Israel's summons to trust the LORD as provider, and it flowers in the New Testament's teaching on contentment and prayer. The watchful-servant parables feed the wider apostolic hope of Christ's sudden return and the accountability of His people.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To form disciples who live honestly and fearlessly before God, who guard their hearts against greed, who trust the Father instead of worrying, who invest in heaven, and who stay awake and faithful until the Master returns at an hour no one can predict.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The parables of the rich fool and the servants describe vivid scenes to press home their point; they are not literal predictions of every believer's death or a timetable for the return. What abides is the prescriptive call to fear God, flee greed, refuse anxiety, seek the kingdom, and watch.

debates.question: Does do not be anxious forbid all planning and provision?

views.label: It forbids faithless worry, not wise stewardship
views.case: Jesus targets the anxious striving of those who live as if there were no Father; the birds still gather and the ancients still farmed, so the command is against corroding worry, not against responsible work and provision.

views.label: It calls for radical dependence bordering on imprudence
views.case: Some hear sell your possessions and consider the ravens as a call to abandon storing up altogether, trusting God day by day; rightly held, this guards the heart from greed without denying honest labor.

debates.bearing: Either way the heart is the issue: seek first the kingdom, hold possessions loosely, and let trust in the Father, not accumulation, be the ground of security.

debates.question: Who is the faithful and wise manager, and who is the servant cut to pieces?

views.label: A warning to leaders and to all disciples
views.case: Prompted by Peter's question, Jesus addresses those given charge, but the principle, readiness and faithfulness, applies to everyone entrusted with the Master's household and word.

views.label: A sobering picture of false and true servants
views.case: The contrast between the watchful servant and the one who beats his fellows and gets drunk distinguishes genuine, ready faith from a nominal profession that presumes on the Master's delay.

debates.bearing: To whom much is given, much is required; the return of the Master will reveal and reward genuine, watchful faithfulness and expose presumption.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter names the dangers plainly. The leaven to beware is hypokrisis, the mask of the play-actor, futile because nothing will stay hidden. The heart's snare is pleonexia, the grasping greed that measures life by possessions and that ruins the rich fool. Against these Jesus sets the refusal of merimnao, the anxious worry that pulls the soul apart over food and clothing, replacing it with trust in a Father who feeds ravens and clothes lilies. And the mark of the waiting people is gregoreo, to stay awake and alert, dressed for service with lamps burning, so that when the Master knocks the door is opened at once.

typology.type: The master who returns and serves his watchful servants
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who came as one who serves and will come again to seat and wait upon His faithful people at His table, Luke 22:27 and John 13:4-5.

typology.type: The baptism of distress Jesus must undergo
typology.fulfillment: The cross, the suffering into which Jesus is plunged to accomplish redemption, Mark 10:38-39.

typology.type: The kingdom given to the little flock
typology.fulfillment: The inheritance the Father is pleased to give His people in Christ, an unfading treasure kept in heaven, 1 Peter 1:4.

study.christ: Jesus stands at the center as the One whose acknowledgment decides all: whoever confesses Him before men He will confess before the angels of God. He is the returning Master of the parables, whose hour of coming is hidden, and who, astonishingly, girds Himself to serve the servants He finds awake, the very posture He took at the supper and takes toward His people still. He speaks of a baptism of anguish He longs to accomplish, the cross before Him even as He journeys to Jerusalem, and of the fire He came to cast on the earth. To watch for Him is to wait for the Servant-King who first served us to death and rose again.
study.god: The God of this chapter is a Father of astonishing attentiveness: not one sparrow is forgotten before Him, the very hairs of our heads are numbered, the ravens are fed and the lilies clothed more gloriously than Solomon. He knows what His children need and is pleased, His good pleasure again, to give the little flock the kingdom. Yet He is also the God to be feared above all who can kill the body, the One before whom every hidden thing will be disclosed and to whom every entrusted servant must give account. Trust and reverent fear meet in the God who is both tender Father and holy Judge.

crossReferences.note: The parallel teaching against anxiety, ending in seek first the kingdom of God and His righteousness.
Greed is named as idolatry, the covetousness Jesus warns against in the rich fool.
Be anxious for nothing, but in everything by prayer bring your requests to God, the cure for worry.
The day of the Lord comes like a thief; therefore keep awake and sober, echoing the watchful servants.

study.oneStory: As Jesus presses on toward the cross, He trains His people to live in the light of His return. He strips away the mask of hypocrisy, guards the heart against greed, and lifts the weight of anxiety by pointing to a Father who feeds the ravens and clothes the lilies and delights to give the kingdom. He calls His little flock to store treasure in heaven and keep their lamps burning, for the Servant-King who was baptized into suffering for them will come again at an hour they do not expect, and blessed are the servants He finds on watch.

sermon.title: It Is Your Father's Pleasure to Give You the Kingdom
sermon.why: Piper preaches from Luke 12, drawing out the Father's glad pleasure to give the little flock the kingdom as the antidote to fear and anxiety over possessions. A faithful, Christ-centered handling of the heart of this movement's call to trust rather than worry.

quiz.q: Whom did Jesus say His disciples should fear?

options.text: God, who has authority over both body and soul, rather than those who can only kill the body.
options.note: Luke 12:4-5 redirects fear from men to God alone.

options.text: The rulers and authorities who could drag them before synagogues.
options.note: These are exactly the ones Jesus says not to fear; the Spirit will help them, so God alone is to be feared.

options.text: The Pharisees, whose leaven of hypocrisy was spreading.
options.note: They are to beware the Pharisees' hypocrisy, but the fear Jesus commands is directed to God, not to the Pharisees.

options.text: No one at all, since fear has no place in faith.
options.note: Jesus explicitly says, fear Him, directing a right and reverent fear toward God.

quiz.q: Why was the rich man in the parable called a fool?

options.text: He stored up treasure for himself but was not rich toward God, and his life was required that night.
options.note: Luke 12:20-21 gives exactly this reason.

options.text: He built bigger barns to store his abundant crops.
options.note: Building barns was the visible act, but the folly was hoarding for himself while poor toward God, not farming or storing as such.

options.text: He failed to share the inheritance with his brother.
options.note: That was the questioner's dispute, not the rich man's sin in the parable.

options.text: He worked too hard and neglected rest.
options.note: His plan was to take it easy; the problem was godless self-reliance, not overwork.

quiz.q: What reason does Jesus give that His disciples need not be anxious?

options.text: Their Father feeds the ravens and clothes the lilies and knows what they need, so they should seek His kingdom.
options.note: Luke 12:24-31 grounds freedom from worry in the Father's care and the priority of the kingdom.

options.text: Worrying cannot add a single hour to one's life.
options.note: True and stated in the passage, but the deeper reason is the Father's provision and the call to seek His kingdom.

options.text: Food and clothing do not matter to God at all.
options.note: They do matter; the Father knows you need them, which is why anxiety is needless, not because needs are unimportant.

options.text: Wealth will always come to those who trust God.
options.note: Jesus promises the kingdom and the Father's care, not guaranteed riches.

quiz.q: What does the parable of the waiting servants teach?

options.text: To stay dressed for service with lamps burning, ready to open at once, because the Master returns at an unexpected hour.
options.note: Luke 12:35-40 calls for watchful readiness for the Son of Man's coming.

options.text: That the Master will reward those found doing their work faithfully.
options.note: True of the faithful manager, but the parable's main thrust is watchful readiness for an unexpected return.

options.text: That the Master's return can be predicted by careful calculation.
options.note: The opposite: He comes at an hour you do not expect, so readiness, not prediction, is required.

options.text: That servants may relax since the Master delays.
options.note: That is the presumption of the wicked servant, whom the Master judges.

quiz.q: What did Jesus rebuke the crowds for failing to interpret?

options.text: The present time, the significance of the hour in which He had come, though they could read the weather.
options.note: Luke 12:54-56 contrasts reading the sky with failing to read the present time.

options.text: The signs of the coming weather from clouds and wind.
options.note: They read these well; their failure was not interpreting the present time, the moment of His coming.

options.text: The written law of Moses and the prophets.
options.note: The issue here is discerning the present hour, not a failure to read the Scriptures.

options.text: The exact day and hour of the final judgment.
options.note: Jesus faults them for missing the significance of His present coming, not for failing to date the judgment.

quiz.q: What does from everyone who has been given much, much will be required teach?

options.text: Greater knowledge of the Master's will brings greater accountability for faithful readiness.
options.note: Luke 12:47-48 ties responsibility to what one has been given and entrusted.

options.text: Those who sin knowingly will be judged more severely than those who do not.
options.note: True from the many and few blows, but the summarizing principle is broader: much given means much required.

options.text: God gives more only to those who already have much.
options.note: The saying is about accountability for what is entrusted, not a rule about who receives more.

options.text: Only leaders, not ordinary disciples, will be held accountable.
options.note: Everyone entrusted with the Master's word is accountable, not leaders alone.

questions: Jesus says nothing hidden will stay hidden. If your private words and thoughts were proclaimed from the housetops, where would you most long for grace?
The rich fool planned for many years and had no tomorrow. How would knowing your days are in God's hands reshape the way you hold your money and plans?
Consider the ravens and the lilies. What is the particular worry that most steals your peace, and what would it mean to entrust it to your Father this week?
Where your treasure is, your heart will be also. What does the way you spend and give reveal about where your treasure actually lies?
The blessed servant is found on watch, lamp burning, when the Master knocks. If Christ returned today, what in your life would you be glad He found, and what would you want to set right first?

translationNotes.note: Hell renders the Greek Gehenna, the valley of Hinnom outside Jerusalem, used as an image of final judgment. The warning is to fear God, who alone has authority over the soul's eternal destiny.

Add a single hour to his life can also be rendered add a single cubit to his height, as the BSB footnote notes; either way the point is that worry cannot achieve even the smallest change, so it is futile.
Seek His kingdom follows the earliest manuscripts; some later ones read the kingdom of God, harmonizing with Matthew 6:33. The BSB records the variant in a footnote; the sense is unchanged.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 12:1-59. Headings, line breaks, footnote markers, and poem markers were skipped when building verse text; em and en dashes were converted to commas or clean breaks. Sermon verified this session from the trusted cluster, John Piper on Luke 12 at desiringgod.org.
